votes.reason: The Python code faithfully implements the custom recursive string splitting, joining, and insertion sort algorithms defined in Coq. The specification logic (checking permutation, sortedness, and reconstruction) is translated accurately, including the use of `reversed` to match `fold_right` behavior.
The Python code faithfully translates all Coq definitions, including the custom string splitting, sorting, and permutation checks, ensuring identical behavior on concrete inputs.
The Python code faithfully translates all Coq definitions, including the string splitting, sorting, and permutation checks, preserving the exact logic of the specification.

The Python code faithfully implements all the Coq definitions. The sorted_by_ascii function uses insertion sort via fold_right (simulated by iterating reversed list), split_by_space handles space character (ASCII 32) identically, join_with_space concatenates with spaces, and anti_shuffle_spec checks the same three conditions: equal word counts, each output word is a sorted permutation of the corresponding input word, and the result equals the joined sorted words. The is_sorted_by_ascii and is_permutation checks are executable versions of the Coq Prop definitions that return the same judgments on concrete inputs.

The Python code faithfully implements the Coq specification. The sorted_by_ascii function uses insertion sort via fold_right (simulated by iterating reversed list), split_by_space handles space character (ASCII 32) identically, join_with_space concatenates with spaces the same way, and anti_shuffle_spec checks the same three conditions: equal word counts, each output word is a sorted permutation of the corresponding input word, and the result equals the joined sorted words. All helper functions (char_to_nat, is_permutation, is_sorted_by_ascii, string_to_list, list_to_string, sort_word) produce equivalent concrete results.

The Python code faithfully implements the Coq specification. The sorted_by_ascii function uses insertion sort via fold_right (simulated by iterating reversed list), split_by_space handles space character (ASCII 32) identically, join_with_space concatenates with spaces, and anti_shuffle_spec checks the same three conditions: equal word counts, each output word is a sorted permutation of the input word, and result equals the joined sorted words. All helper functions (char_to_nat, is_permutation, is_sorted_by_ascii, string_to_list, list_to_string, sort_word) produce equivalent concrete results.

The Python functions mirror the Coq spec on ASCII strings: same insertion sort by ord, same split/join by spaces (preserving empty words), permutation via counts, and the anti_shuffle_spec checks the same conditions including exact expected result.

Python mirrors the Coq functions: ord matches nat_of_ascii on ASCII, insertion sort via reversed-fold equals fold_right insert, split/join by space behave identically, and the spec checks the same conjunction (lengths, per-word permutation and sortedness, and equality to joined sorted words). Thus it agrees on all ASCII inputs.

Helper functions (char_to_nat, sorted_by_ascii via insertion, is_sorted_by_ascii, is_permutation, split/join by space, conversions) mirror Coq behavior, and anti_shuffle_spec checks the same length, per-word permutation/sortedness, and final equality. Matches on all ASCII inputs.
